perturbation.original_text: 8. LIABILITY; INDEMNITY 8.1 The Parties shall be liable for non-execution and/or improper execution of their obligations under this Agreement in accordance with the legislation of the Republic of Kazakstan.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. LIABILITY; INDEMNITY 8.1 The Parties' liability for non-execution and/or improper execution of their obligations under this Agreement shall be limited to the contract price.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting liability to the contract price may contradict laws in the Republic of Kazakhstan regarding the extent of liability for breach of contract, potentially violating regulations on fair compensation for damages.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Civil Code of the Republic of Kazakhstan, Article 350
perturbation.law_citation: Article 350 of the Civil Code of the Republic of Kazakhstan
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 350 of the Civil Code of the Republic of Kazakhstan states that a debtor is obliged to compensate the creditor for losses caused by failure to fulfill or improper fulfillment of an obligation. Limiting liability to the contract price might not fully compensate for all direct losses, thus contradicting Article 350.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text states that the parties will be liable for non-execution or improper execution of obligations in accordance with the legislation of the Republic of Kazakhstan. The changed text limits this liability to the contract price. The Law Explanation states that Article 350 of the Civil Code of the Republic of Kazakhstan requires a debtor to compensate the creditor for losses caused by failure to fulfill an obligation, and that limiting liability to the contract price might not fully compensate for all direct losses. The modified text contradicts this legal requirement by limiting the scope of liability, potentially violating the creditor's right to full compensation as stated in Article 350. The scraped law snippet is irrelevant as it pertains to housing payments.

perturbation.original_text: 11. SETTLEMENT OF DISPUTES AND APPLICABLE LAW 11.1 In the event of any disputes arousal under this Contract, the Parties shall exercise all reasonable efforts to resolve them by negotiations. 11.2 In the event that resolution of the disputes by negotiations is impossible, they shall be subject to court consideration at the defendant's location. 11.3 Effective legislation of the Republic of Kazakhstan shall apply to any relations of the Parties arising out of this Contract.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. SETTLEMENT OF DISPUTES AND APPLICABLE LAW 11.1 In the event of any disputes arousal under this Contract, the Parties shall exercise all reasonable efforts to resolve them by negotiations. 11.2 In the event that resolution of the disputes by negotiations is impossible, they shall be subject to court consideration at the defendant's location. 11.3 The laws of England shall apply to any relations of the Parties arising out of this Contract.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the applicable law from the "Effective legislation of the Republic of Kazakhstan" to the "laws of England" in Article 11.3 may violate mandatory provisions of Kazakhstan law, particularly those relating to contractual obligations performed within Kazakhstan, or involving Kazakh entities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Civil Code of the Republic of Kazakhstan, Article 1112
perturbation.law_citation: Article 1112 of the Civil Code of the Republic of Kazakhstan
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1112 of the Civil Code of the Republic of Kazakhstan stipulates that Kazakhstan law applies to civil law relations with a foreign element, including those arising from contracts, where the place of performance is Kazakhstan or where a party is a Kazakh legal entity. Choosing English law could contradict mandatory provisions designed to protect the interests of Kazakh entities.

perturbation.justification: The change from Kazakhstan law to English law directly contradicts the principle that Kazakhstan law should govern contracts performed within Kazakhstan or involving Kazakh entities, as explained in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation also indicates a likely violation of mandatory provisions. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is about changes to housing payment regulations and is not directly applicable, the Law Explanation states that Article 1112 of the Civil Code of the Republic of Kazakhstan dictates Kazakhstan law for contracts performed there or involving Kazakh legal entities, which would be undermined by applying English law instead.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 At all times during the term of this Contract, the Principal shall: (i) Deliver Karakuduk Commodity to the Company at Metering Point 719 (PSP Samara of the Western branch office of KazTransOil) in the amount determined in accordance with item 1.2 of this Agreement; (ii) independently conclude contracts with the Buyer on selling Crude Oil for export and submit a copy of each such contract with the Buyer to the Company 5 days before the beginning of the relevant Delivery Month; (iii) obtain, at its own expense, required official export permissions (certificate of origin of the Commodity at place of the load output, customs declaration on Commodity output) for release of the batch of Commodity being delivered from the customs territory of the Republic of Kazakstan and deliver them before the 5th day of the Delivery Month;
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 At all times during the term of this Contract, the Principal shall: (i) Deliver Karakuduk Commodity to the Company at Metering Point 719 (PSP Samara of the Western branch office of KazTransOil) in the amount determined in accordance with item 1.2 of this Agreement; (ii) independently conclude contracts with the Buyer on selling Crude Oil for export and submit a copy of each such contract with the Buyer to the Company 5 days before the beginning of the relevant Delivery Month;
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement in 4.2(iii) for the Principal to obtain and deliver export permissions may contradict regulations of the Republic of Kazakhstan regarding export controls and customs procedures. This is because export permissions are required by law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Law of the Republic of Kazakhstan on Export Control, Article 8
perturbation.law_citation: Article 8 of the Law of the Republic of Kazakhstan on Export Control
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 8 of the Law of the Republic of Kazakhstan on Export Control states that entities engaged in export operations are responsible for obtaining the necessary permits and licenses. Removing the obligation in the contract contradicts this legal requirement, potentially leading to illegal export activities and liabilities for the involved parties.

Министерству экономики Республики Казахстан внести в установленном порядке на рассмотрение Правительства Республики Казахстан: 1) в срок до 15 декабря 1999 года проект постановления Правительства Республики Казахстан по утверждению Правил разработки и реализации индикативных планов социально-экономического развития Республики Казахстан; Поставить закладку Посмотреть закладки Добавить комментарий 2) до 1 января 2000 года Концепцию социально-экономического развития Республики Казахстан на 2001-2005 годы и прогноз важнейших макроэкономических показателей; Поставить закладку Посмотреть закладки Добавить комментарий Подпункт 3) пункта 1 изложен в новой редакции в соответствии с постановлением Правительства РК от 10.05.2000 г. № 691 (см. старую редакцию ) Показать изменения 3) совместно с центральными и местными исполнительными органами до 1 августа 2000 года проект Индикативного плана социально-экономического развития Республики Казахстан на 2001 - 2005 годы. perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated that the Principal obtain and deliver export permissions, while the modified contract removes this requirement. The Law Explanation states that Article 8 of the Law of the Republic of Kazakhstan on Export Control requires entities engaged in export operations to obtain the necessary permits and licenses. The Perturbation Explanation confirms that export permissions are required by law in Kazakhstan. The omission of this obligation directly contradicts the legal requirement for obtaining export permissions, as asserted in the Law Explanation.
